speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 2, and we're interviewing Albert Tausinger. Albert, tell me about your secondary school that you went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: The secondary school?

speaker: interviewer
text: The second school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The business academy, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the business school.

speaker: interviewee
text: The business-- it was a secondary school. You must go up to 14 years, yes. After 14 years, you go out from the school or go further in the higher school. Yes, in the middle school. So I go to the, uh, business academy. It wasn't in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was in [NON-ENGLISH], Not far from [NON-ENGLISH]. So I go with the train every morning there. And in the afternoon, home. And after four years, I finish. Yes. I go to-- I [INAUDIBLE]. In English, the [INAUDIBLE]. Yes? The certificate. And after I started to work in the bank.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just a minute. Let's just go back to that school. Was that a state school or a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. State school.

speaker: interviewer
text: A state school.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish school or [INAUDIBLE] school was only the first four years. Not [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So were most of your friends in this school Jewish or not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school? No, it was very mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So when you left school, did you know what you wanted to do? What was your ambition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ambition? To work. To earn money. You know? So I started after [NON-ENGLISH]. I finished school. It was in June. So I started, what I told you, in the [INAUDIBLE] insurance. And after, I changed in the [INAUDIBLE] Bank. So the [INAUDIBLE] insurance, it was Viennese, Austrian insurance company. They have a lot of these in, uh, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when you started work?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old I was? I was nearly 19. Yes, 19. And, uh, I work up to '31, 1931. So, and after in Czechoslovakia, in Moscow, in the army. So I was [CLEARS THROAT] in the army 18 months. You know? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go into the army, tell me a bit about the work. Did you like working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work in [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always in [NON-ENGLISH]. Working. [INAUDIBLE] you must work in Saturday up to 1 o'clock. And from 1 o'clock, I run out for [INAUDIBLE]. Or in winter, for skiing. You know? It wasn't so-- and the working time was different. I don't remember. We started half past 7:00 in the morning. Yes, 8 o'clock, and finished 6 o'clock in the afternoon with a half-hour break. You know? So when you go, you take some sandwich or something with you. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you meet friends after work? Did you belong to any groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, [NON-ENGLISH], it was a small town. So we always see each other or in the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, or in a cafeteria, you know? In the evening, they play some cards or just drink coffee and talk [INAUDIBLE]. Yes. And then Sunday was the so-called 5 o'clock tea. So they come, the girls, and we dance from 5:00 to 7:00. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a special girlfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: A special f-- girlfriend? I had-- yes. Now, she was a daughter from a very rich pharmacist. They had a pharmacy. And I was a son from a poor family, so he don't like my friendship to her. So I see her always after the school or so [INAUDIBLE]. The parents don't like me. I see her again after the war. She was married and I was married, too. It was my so-called 'big childhood love.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was [? Katherine ?] too, and [INAUDIBLE]. Jewish girls. I don't know the parents was, uh, [INAUDIBLE] in Auschwitz. I don't know how she-- how I say? To stay, you know? She was hiding around the [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So tell me about the army. What was it like in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean before the war or after?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before it was normal. You must-- everybody, every man in Czechoslovakia, when he started 20, he must go in the army. Yes. Then you-- normally, not educated, not [INAUDIBLE]. They must stay 24 months. And people who were the [INAUDIBLE], they stay 18 months. Yes? So I was in the school. You must go in the school. And I finished the army. I was a sergeant. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience any anti-Semitism in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the army, not. I can't say. I don't really say there wasn't anti-Semites. You know? No. I-- so, directly, I don't, uh, feel it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you pleased to leave the army or not? Or did you want to stay in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was always anti-army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't like the uniform. I don't like the top discipline. You know? Every step you must have a-- somebody must give you permission. You can do that or not. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happens? Sunday, you know, there was an organization. The Catholics goes from 11:00-- from 10 o'clock up to mid-day to the church. So I always go between them to the church and [INAUDIBLE] run home. You know? [LAUGHS] Again, 12 o'clock I come with them back to the army, you know? So we make some tricks, dirty tricks, however you say. You know, to be a few hours, uh, free, you know? And did you have any special friends in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They was all around. And interestingly, in [NON-ENGLISH], in the army where I was, it was [INAUDIBLE]. [CLEARS THROAT] [INAUDIBLE]. There were a lot of Sudeten German. You know? There's Sender-- Sudeten German from Czech part, from Bohemia, to the Slovakian. Not-- don't leave them at home. So they're mixed up, the Sudeten German. It was-- I can't tell you about the direct anti-Semitism. Anti-Semitism [? was, ?] I feel, in Poland. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: After?

speaker: interviewee
text: After.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell-- tell me, did you speak German in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the army, only Slovak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Only Slovak.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slovak. Or, mostly, the officers there was Czechs. So they speaked mixed Slovak-Czech. It's very-- very close, you know, these two languages. It's no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you have to do to become a sergeant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal. I can, uh-- do the [INAUDIBLE] and the command and this. And when comes the new one, I command them. You know? A small group, you know? Not-- nothing so special.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do when you left the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left the army in 1933, beginning of '34-- I don't remember. I started again working. [? Now, ?] it was difficult to go back to the bank. They don't took me back. You know? So I started in some private, uh, firms to work-- help. And I started work at home. You know, my father had a business. Actually, not my father. It was my oldest brothers. They have a business. They sell corks. You know what cork is? In the bottle, what you put.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes? So I work partly at home, partly help in different firms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did the bank not take you back?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't more of [INAUDIBLE]. You know? They don't guarantee you after the-- not the war, the army that they'll take you back. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like working for your brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was nice. You know? And [CLEARS THROAT] it was a-- in the beginning, it was very hard, very difficult. My father, he wasn't-- he was a very good employee, my father, like I am. But he wasn't a businessman, you know? I am a very good worker. I am not a businessman. You know? It is my oldest brother-- the two oldest-- that was a good businessman. Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: The [INAUDIBLE] the brother in Israel they weren't the same-- not businessman. They were worker. So it was in the family, so [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGH] mixed up. So and I was working up to '39. You know? Can I f-- Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Up to '39. My mother, she died-- some trouble with her heart-- in 1938. So I stayed at home with my father and for me older brother. He was married, had a little daughter. So we stay in the house-- yes. My father, I, and [INAUDIBLE] up to 1939. You know? In 1939, I was in Prague for something for the business-- I don't remember. And it was-- come Hitler in-- in Czechoslovakia. Actually, in Prague. It was in March 1939. I was in the [NON-ENGLISH] Square and have seen the German come in. With the war, nothing. They occupate. And they split Czechoslovakia in [INAUDIBLE] Czech and Bohemia-- Bohemia and [INAUDIBLE]. The Czech and [INAUDIBLE]. And Slovakia was a so-called 'independent state,' a Slovak state. Yes. In Europe, in [INAUDIBLE] was only two-- under the German control, two independent state. It was Slovakia and [NON-ENGLISH]. Only these two states in Europe. All other was occupate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I come back with two friends. I say, I don't stay here under the Slovak. And actually, [INAUDIBLE] started anti-Semitism. They started to talk to Jewish businesses, you know, the Slovaks. What-- what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do? What did they do? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: They take away from the Jewish their business. Yeah? Throw out. You know? And so I found two friends, Jewish, and I leave Slovakia. Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: I go through the border between Poland and-- Slovakia and Poland to come to [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland. And there, I was registered. It was a committee, you know, for immigrants. Mostly, the immigrants was from Czechoslovakia and Austria. Not from Hungarian-- mostly from Czechoslovakia and [INAUDIBLE] that was registered. And I was-- it was in April. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wait for the visa to Britain, you know, via the committee. So no visa, no visa, no visa. You know, always when I ask the committee for my visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, after the war, you know, I must tell you one letter changed all my life. What happens? The visa was there. My sister here found out in the home office that I have a visa. Now, it was registered under not Tausinger, T for Thomas. No, under R, Robert. Not Tausinger, Rausinger. So I [INAUDIBLE]. And this letter changed all my life. You know? I stay in [NON-ENGLISH]. I [CLEARS THROAT] sleep in a-- [? how do say? ?] Where the people sleep, you know? These-- I don't-- I forgot the name. One night comes the Polish police, and take us all away-- the Jewish, you know? In German, you say [GERMAN]. In Slovakian. In English, it's-- no, in England, over Christmas, the people who stay in the street, they take it for a few nights in a hostel. You know? They took us on the German border. You know? Me, my friends, [INAUDIBLE]. I was lucky. You know? I was hiding in the little [INAUDIBLE]. You know? And my friends, they take the gestapo. So I go away back and stay near to [NON-ENGLISH]. And when I come to [NON-ENGLISH], I don't remember how I found an older Jewish couple, and they took me, give me-- uh, I can sleep there. So and I wait, wait, wait, again, for my visa. No visa. No visa, up to nearly September. You know? 19-- [CLEARS THROAT] 1939, you know? How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: September 1939? 26 also. We [INAUDIBLE] started the war between Poland and-- and Germany. So one way where we can go, it was only to the east to Romania. You know? In the '39, it was only central Europe. State was Romania. It wasn't under German control. Hungarias was under German control.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we run, I think, three days-- Run, run, run, run-- to so-called [NON-ENGLISH]. West [NON-ENGLISH]. You know West [NON-ENGLISH] belonged from 1918 to Poland. Yes? No. It was-- it was Ukraine, Ukraine-speaking people. No, they belonged to [NON-ENGLISH]. So after three days, or how much, I don't remember, I was in [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a small town [? in this. ?] In the morning, I wake up. I was in the Soviet Union. You know? What happens? The Soviets, you know, in September, 1939, they cross the border and occupate all the West [NON-ENGLISH]. So I wake up. I was in the Soviet Union. Actually, they crossed the border. You know? It was the [INAUDIBLE] line, yes, from the Finland up to the [INAUDIBLE]. You know, it was in 1918, from France. You know? So I was in the Soviet Union. Can I just stop you there? I want to take you back a little bit, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to take you back first when you were in Prague--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In [NON-ENGLISH] Square and Hitler marched in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I nearly fell over. How I can feel when you see the German-- you know, you know, these German walking, the army. You know? It was-- it's difficult to say what you feel. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And after, when you talk-- when I come back to [NON-ENGLISH], you know, from Prague, before I run away, you know, I sit in a small pub. You know? And it was the biggest trouble. And after, come two German officers, you know, and said to me, you know, I was a little bit arrogant. You know? I tell-- tell them, sorry. It is not-- I think you don't like it. I am a Jewish. And they look to me. [INAUDIBLE] what, you are talking rubbish. You are a Jewish with blue eyes and blond hair? You can't be a Jewish. So I said, sorry. [LAUGH] I am a Jewish. Forget it. So I go away. It was the first moment. You know? So what I'm thinking to run away. You know? It's not a future for me. How did it feel saying goodbye to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, again, a hard thing. I don't [? told ?] him I go away. I told only my brother-- yes. The one brother, he was there. I told him I go with these two friends through the-- in Germany, you say through the green border. You know? Through the night. And so I don't know in English how you call it. It is the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Illegal. I-- I crossed the border illegal, without documents [INAUDIBLE]. So when I was in Poland and come to the Russian, started again the other troubles. You know? First, tell me about running away. Where did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I running?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes, somebody take you for the night. Let-- sometime, all the night, I stay up. Not in the street, somewhere around. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And for food? What did you do for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: For food? We had some food from the committee. We was a big group. I wasn't al-- alone. You know, when we run from [NON-ENGLISH] to the east, we had in fo-- some food. You know, the-- they take a cart with horses, you know? And they take for us-- but what was the food? Nothing. Some bread and [INAUDIBLE]. You know? So--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you said your friends were taken by the gestapo and you were hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in hiding. And they go back. They take them to Slovakia. And after, they died in the Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One was Greenwald. And the other was Santo, Fritz Santo. And Greenwald-- there was three brothers. I've forgotten the name. Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: So when they were taken, you were by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was by myself. So I go around back to [NON-ENGLISH] and hide again by these Jewish family. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, there-- you know, the Polish, when they take us to the German, to the [INAUDIBLE] border, they take us all around. You know? Throw us to the border, you know, to the Germans. I was separated from the friends. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you very scared? Frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. Yes. You run away from the Germans and they took you illegally back to the Germans. Sure, I was frightened. It is why I hide myself and-- and go back, with hoping to come to Britain. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister was already in Britain?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, she was lucky. She was with her husband before '39, you know? They go with a train. They got a visa to England in Prague. So they come without Poland straight to-- to Britain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which sister is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name? The sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: For my sister? It was Katerina [NON-ENGLISH] Tausinger.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one that was four years older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years. She was in [? 19 ?] [? 8. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your other brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: They stay in Slovakia, you know, up to-- I don't know in which year. Started the Slovak-- made a camp, you know? They concentrate all the-- all the Jewish people. You know? Not only from [NON-ENGLISH], from all the Slovakia. And from there, you know, from this camp, they're taken in a-- with a train to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So nobody else in your family tried to run away or to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was [? older brother. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's just stop there and we'll change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? All right. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
